elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the low Reynolds number hydrodynamics of a spherical swimmer with a predominantly hydrophobic surface, except for a hydrophilic active patch.
elements.sent-2411-002: This active patch covers a portion of the surface and exhibits chiral activity that varies as a function of $\theta$ and $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our study considers two types of active patches: (i) a symmetric active patch (independent of $\phi$) and (ii) an arbitrary active patch (depends on both $\theta$ and $\phi$).
elements.sent-2411-004: The swimming velocity, rotation rate, and flow field of the swimmer are calculated analytically.
elements.sent-2411-005: The objective of this work is to find the optimal configurations for both patch models to maximize the swimmer's velocity and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-006: Interestingly, the maximum velocity can be controlled by adjusting the hydrophobicity, patch configuration, and strength of the surface activity.
elements.sent-2411-007: We find that for the symmetric patch model, the swimmer's velocity is $U_{SP = 1.414 U_s$, where $U_s$ is the velocity of a swimmer whose surface is fully covered with chiral activity as a reference.}
elements.sent-2411-008: For the arbitrary patch model, the velocity is $U_{AP = 1.45 U_s$, which is higher than that of the symmetric patch model.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Our results indicate that swimmers with low hydrophobicity exhibit efficient swimming characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-010: Additionally, due to the incomplete coverage of the active patch, the Stokeslet and Rotlet terms appear in the flow field generated by the swimmer, which is a deviation compared to the case of a swimmer whose surface is fully covered with chiral activity.
elements.sent-2411-011: This study provides insights useful for designing synthetic active particles, which can be applied, for example, in targeted drug delivery, chemotaxis, and phototaxis.
elements.sent-2411-012: The study of active swimmers is an emerging field of interest \cite{lauga2009hydrodynamics,vicsek2012collective,marchetti2013hydrodynamics, Eric_book .}
elements.sent-2411-013: A significant portion of these studies is predicated on the notion that each particle moves with a velocity determined by its activity and the ambient fluid flow.
elements.sent-2411-014: Various models have been proposed to study the self-propelled motion of diverse microorganisms \cite{Eric_book .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Many theoretical, experimental, and numerical studies concentrated on shapes, surface properties, hydrodynamic interaction, and collective behaviors of microorganisms~\citep{stone1996propulsion,pedley2016spherical,theers2016modeling,liu2022migration .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Microorganisms that swim inhabit an environment where viscous forces govern their movements.
elements.sent-2411-017: Lighthill \cite{lighthill1952squirming and Blake \cite{blake1971spherical first proposed the squirmer model to study the propulsion of ciliated microorganisms.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: When the surface of a motile microorganism is densely covered with beating cilia, e.g., {\it{Paramecium and {\it{Opalina , it generates metachronal waves, which cause an active slip velocity over the surface of the microorganism \cite{blake1971spherical,machemer1972ciliary .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Apart from this, there are other ciliated microorganisms, such as {\it{Marine Zooplankton and {\it{Acineta Protozoa , which have cilia at a particular region on their surface \cite{gibbons1981cilia .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Willmott \cite{willmott2008dynamics,willmott2009slip studied the slip-induced dynamics of a Janus-like sphere in Stokes flow, focusing on binary discontinuous boundary conditions between slip and no-slip regions.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The resulting flow asymmetry due to these discontinuous boundary conditions was analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-022: Dhar et al. \cite{dhar2019self later examined the hydrodynamics of a rigid slip-stick swimmer at low Reynolds number, with active slip on an axisymmetric patch and Navier slip on the rest of the surface.}
elements.sent-2411-023: They found that adjusting the patch configuration could enhance the swimmer's migration velocity by up to 50\% compared to a fully covered swimmer.
elements.sent-2411-024: In our recent work \cite{biswas2023hydrodynamics , we explored a spherical swimmer in arbitrary Stokes flow with a non-axisymmetric patch dividing the surface into distinct slip regions.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We showed that varying the patch configuration and slip length ratio effectively controls locomotion.
elements.sent-2411-026: The resulting jump in the boundary condition creates localized effects near the swimmer, influencing streamline deviations.
elements.sent-2411-027: Both studies identify optimal patch configurations that maximize swimming velocity.
elements.sent-2411-028: More recently, Yang et al. \cite{yang2023stick studied a spherical swimmer with hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions and axisymmetric surface activity.}
elements.sent-2411-029: They demonstrated that, by selecting appropriate swimming modes and cap shape, a swimmer with a hydrophobic cap can outperform a fully hydrophilic swimmer in efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-030: These investigations provide insight into the dynamics of active particles with regionally confined surface activity.
elements.sent-2411-031: To our knowledge, there is no experimental study on the fabrication technique to implement artificial cilia over a portion of the surface of solid particles.
elements.sent-2411-032: However, few studies are based on the simulation of multiple cilia concentrated over the spherical surface to investigate microbial locomotion \cite{omori2020swimming,rode2021multi,westwood2021coordinated .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Apart from this, there are studies on the locomotion of self-propelled droplets fully or partially covered with a stagnant surfactant layer \cite{sadhal1983stokes,pak2014viscous,seemann2016self,sharanya2021transient .}
elements.sent-2411-034: There are chemically active microswimmers such as the electrophoretic colloidal particles (\cite{anderson1989colloid ), dielectric spheres partially covered by Pt \cite{howse2007self,ebbens2012size,brown2014ionic or the Au-Pt Janus particles \cite{lee2014self .}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: These swimmers move mostly because of different phoretic effects \cite{moran2017phoretic .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The phoretic motion of a spherical particle where surface reactants are distributed asymmetrically is studied by Golestanian et al. \cite{golestanian2005propulsion .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Consecutively, by taking into account the activity and mobility, the phoretic motion of the colloidal particle has been studied when these are distributed symmetrically \cite{golestanian2007designing and asymmetrically \cite{lisicki2018autophoretic over the particle.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: In this study, one of the considered spherical particle's surface is partitioned into two different regions by a chemically active cap, and the other region is inert.
elements.sent-2411-039: For this, the binary-valued phoretic mobility has been incorporated.
elements.sent-2411-040: As a result, discontinuity in the phoretic surface active slip arises.
elements.sent-2411-041: The flow field behavior in the laboratory frame of reference due to the chemically active cap has been analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-042: Recently, Burada et al. \cite{burada_pre studied the chiral swimmer where surface active slip velocity is distributed asymmetrically, contributing to both translations and rotations of the motion.}
elements.sent-2411-043: This analogy between the structure of the swimmer's slip and the surface active slip velocity of a model artificial swimmer supports the motivation of the present framework.
elements.sent-2411-044: Based on the literature and observations, we aim to develop a well-controllable spherical chiral swimmer driven by surface active slip concentrated over an arbitrary patch of its surface, with the rest being hydrophobic, characterized by Navier slip length.
elements.sent-2411-045: This study's primary objective is to identify the optimal configuration of the active patch region to maximize the swimmer's velocity strength.
elements.sent-2411-046: The article is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-047: In Section~\ref{sec: math_model , we present our model system, including the relevant boundary conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Section~\ref{sec: Ana_solution outlines our solution methodology.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Section~\ref{sec: axi_model focuses on a swimmer with a symmetric active patch.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In Section~\ref{sec: Naxi_model , we explore the case of a swimmer with an arbitrary active patch and determine the global optimal patch configuration that maximizes the migration velocity of the swimmer.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Finally, the discussion and conclusions are presented in Sections~\ref{sec: discussion and~\ref{sec: conclusions , respectively.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{Fig1.pdf} \caption{(a) Schematic diagram of a spherical swimmer with an arbitrary patch in the region $\mho$, where an active slip $\boldsymbol{v_s}$ is prescribed (b). In the other region $(S - \mho)$, the swimmer surface is hydrophobic with a slip length of $k$. (c) Swimmer with a symmetric patch.} \label{fig: schematic}
elements.sent-2411-052: We consider a spherical swimmer of radius $a$ in a quiescent ambient viscous fluid with viscosity $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-053: We assume that the activity on the surface is restricted to a patch region (refer to figure~\ref{fig: schematic (a,b)).}
elements.sent-2411-054: In figure~\ref{fig: schematic (c), we show the corresponding model with a surface activity prescribed on a strip as a symmetric patch.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We denote the patch region as $\mho$ and the rest of the surface of the swimmer as $S-\mho$, where $S$ denotes the surface of the spherical swimmer.
elements.sent-2411-056: We further assume that the region $S-\mho$ is hydrophobic with a Navier slip length $k \ll a$.
elements.sent-2411-058: We use the spherical polar coordinates $(r,\theta,\phi)$ coordinates with the corresponding unit vectors $(\boldsymbol{e _r,\boldsymbol{e _\theta,\boldsymbol{e _\phi)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The active patch region is defined as $\mho = \big\{ (r,\theta,\phi) \in S~|~r=a,\theta_{1 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \theta_{2 , \phi_1 \leqslant \phi \leqslant \phi_2 \big\}$, where $\theta_{1 ,\theta_{2}$ are polar and $\phi_{1 ,\phi_{2}$ are azimuthal slip angles (refer to figure~\ref{fig: schematic (b)).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: In the rest of the article, we denote $P^m_n(\cos\theta)$ as $P^m_n$.
elements.sent-2411-062: The boundary conditions at the surface of the swimmer and the far field read as \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:bconditions} \boldsymbol{u} \cdot \boldsymbol{n} = 0, \quad \text{on} \quad S, \label{eqn: impermeable} \boldsymbol{u} \cdot \boldsymbol{t} = \left\{ \begin{array}{rl} \displaystyle \boldsymbol{v_s} \cdot \boldsymbol{t} , \text{on } \mho, \displaystyle \frac{k}{\mu}(\boldsymbol{n}\cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma}) \cdot \boldsymbol{t}, \text{on } S-\mho, \end{array} \right. \label{eqn: slip_condition} \boldsymbol{u} \to -(\boldsymbol{U} + \boldsymbol{\Omega}\times\boldsymbol{r}) \quad \text{as} \quad \boldsymbol{r} \to \infty\, \label{eqn: farfield_condition},\end{eqnarray} where $\boldsymbol{n , \boldsymbol{t}$ are, respectively, the unit normal and tangent vectors to the surface of the swimmer, $\boldsymbol{\sigma = -p\boldsymbol{I + \mu ({\boldsymbol{\nabla \boldsymbol{u +{\boldsymbol{\nabla \boldsymbol{u ^T)$ is the stress tensor, where $\boldsymbol{I}$ is the identity matrix, and $T$ denotes transpose.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:bconditions} u \cdot n = 0, \quad on \quad S, \label{eqn: impermeable} u \cdot t = \left\{ {rl} \displaystyle v_s \cdot t , on  \mho, \displaystyle \frac{k}{\mu}(n\cdot \sigma) \cdot t, on  S-\mho,  \right. \label{eqn: slip_condition} u \to -(U + \Omega\timesr) \quad as \quad r \to \infty\, \label{eqn: farfield_condition},}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-063: Here, $\boldsymbol{u}$ denotes the flow field in the body frame of reference.
elements.sent-2411-064: This is analogous to the studies by Popescu et al.~\cite{popescu2018effective and Yang et al.~\cite{yang2023stick .}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The incompressible steady Stokes equation (Eqn.~\ref{eqn: stokes ), subject to the described boundary conditions, allows for analytical solutions to determine the swimmer's velocity and pressure fields.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Alternatively, for spherical geometries, the double curl method is widely used by several authors~\cite{padmavathi1993stokes,dhar2019self,biswas2023hydrodynamics .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Results due to Almansi \cite{almansi1899sull , we can express $A$ as $A = A_1 + r^2 A_2$, where $A_1$ and $A_2$ are two independent harmonics.}
elements.sent-2411-069: As a result, the representation involves three independent scalar harmonic functions, namely $A_1, A_2$, and $B$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Therefore, the general solutions of ${\boldsymbol{\nabla ^4 A = 0$ and ${\boldsymbol{\nabla ^2 B = 0$ are given by \begin{eqnarray}A = \sum^\infty_{n=1} \Big[a_n r^n +b_n r^{n+2} + \frac{c_n}{r^{n+1}} + \frac{d_n}{r^{n-1}} \Big] S_n(\theta,\phi) \label{eqn: A} B = \sum^\infty_{n=1} \Big[e_n r^n + \frac{f_n}{r^{n+1}} \Big] H_n(\theta,\phi), \label{eqn: B}\end{eqnarray} where $a_n,b_n,c_n,d_n,e_n,f_n$ are the unknown coefficients.}}}}

B = \left\{ \begin{array}{rl} \displaystyle -\sum_n \gamma_n \tilde{H}_n(\theta,\phi), \quad \text{on } \mho, \displaystyle k \Big(\frac{\partial B}{\partial r}-\frac{B}{a}\Big), \quad \text{on } S-\mho, \end{array} \right.\end{eqnarray}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-072: Therefore, the scalar fields in the body frame of reference by incorporating conditions Eqs.~(\ref{eqn: scalar_imp -\ref{eqn: scalar_slip_patch_other ), due to the patch $\mho$ are given as \begin{eqnarray}A_\mho = \Big[r + \frac{a^3}{2 r^2} -\frac{3a}{2} \Big] \Big[-\frac{u_3}{2} P^0_1+\Big(\frac{u_1}{2} \cos \phi+\frac{u_2}{2}\sin \phi\Big)P^1_1\Big] + \Big[\frac{a}{2}-\frac{a^3}{2r^2}\Big]\beta_1 \tilde{S}_1 \nonumber \: + \sum^{\infty}_{n=2} \Big[ \frac{a^n}{2 r^{n-1}} - \frac{a^{n+2}}{2 r^{n+1}} \Big] \beta_n \tilde{S}_n, \label{eqn: Aa} B_\mho = \Big[r - \frac{a^3}{r^2}\Big] \Big[(\Omega_1 \cos \phi+\Omega_2 \sin \phi)P^1_1-\Omega_3 P^0_1\Big] - \gamma_1 \Big(\frac{a}{r}\Big)^{2} \tilde{H}_1 - \sum^{\infty}_{n=2} \gamma_n\Big(\frac{a}{r}\Big)^{n+1} \tilde{H}_n, \qquad \label{eqn: Ba} \quad\end{eqnarray} whereas the same due to the rest of the surface $S-\mho$ is given as \begin{eqnarray}A_{S-\mho} = \Big[a - r +\frac{a^2 \,r^2 - a^4}{2 r^2 (a + 3 k)}\Big] \Big[\frac{u_3}{2} P^0_1 - \left(\frac{u_1}{2} \cos \phi+\frac{u_2}{2}\sin \phi\right) P^1_1\Big], \label{eqn: Ap} B_{S-\mho} = \Big[r - \frac{a^4}{(a + 3 k)r^2}\Big] \Big[-\Omega_3 P^0_1+\Big(\Omega_1 \cos \phi+\Omega_2 \sin \phi\Big)P^1_1\Big]. \label{eqn: Bp}\end{eqnarray} The general solution of the velocity field $\boldsymbol{u}$ can be computed by implementing Eqs.~(\ref{eqn: Aa -\ref{eqn: Bp ) to Eqn.~\ref{eqn: dcu .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}A_\mho = \Big[r + \frac{a^3}{2 r^2} -\frac{3a}{2} \Big] \Big[-\frac{u_3}{2} P^0_1+\Big(\frac{u_1}{2} \cos \phi+\frac{u_2}{2}\sin \phi\Big)P^1_1\Big] + \Big[\frac{a}{2}-\frac{a^3}{2r^2}\Big]\beta_1 \tilde{S}_1 \nonumber \: + \sum^{\infty}_{n=2} \Big[ \frac{a^n}{2 r^{n-1}} - \frac{a^{n+2}}{2 r^{n+1}} \Big] \beta_n \tilde{S}_n, \label{eqn: Aa} B_\mho = \Big[r - \frac{a^3}{r^2}\Big] \Big[(\Omega_1 \cos \phi+\Omega_2 \sin \phi)P^1_1-\Omega_3 P^0_1\Big] - \gamma_1 \Big(\frac{a}{r}\Big)^{2} \tilde{H}_1 - \sum^{\infty}_{n=2} \gamma_n\Big(\frac{a}{r}\Big)^{n+1} \tilde{H}_n, \qquad \label{eqn: Ba} \quad\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}A_{S-\mho} = \Big[a - r +\frac{a^2 \,r^2 - a^4}{2 r^2 (a + 3 k)}\Big] \Big[\frac{u_3}{2} P^0_1 - \left(\frac{u_1}{2} \cos \phi+\frac{u_2}{2}\sin \phi\right) P^1_1\Big], \label{eqn: Ap} B_{S-\mho} = \Big[r - \frac{a^4}{(a + 3 k)r^2}\Big] \Big[-\Omega_3 P^0_1+\Big(\Omega_1 \cos \phi+\Omega_2 \sin \phi\Big)P^1_1\Big]. \label{eqn: Bp}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-077: The net drag $\boldsymbol{D}$ and net torque $\boldsymbol{T}$ of the swimmer can be obtained using the relations $\boldsymbol{D = \int_\mho \boldsymbol{\sigma \cdot \boldsymbol{n ~ dS+\int_{S-\mho \boldsymbol{\sigma \cdot \boldsymbol{n ~ dS$ and $\boldsymbol{T = \int_\mho \boldsymbol{r \times (\boldsymbol{\sigma \cdot \boldsymbol{n ) ~ dS+\int_{S-\mho \boldsymbol{r \times (\boldsymbol{\sigma \cdot \boldsymbol{n ) ~ dS$, where $S$ denotes the surface of the swimmer.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Since the swimmer is suspended in the quiescent fluid without any external forces and torques, its velocity $\boldsymbol{U}$ and rotation rate $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ can be determined from the force-free ($\boldsymbol{D = \boldsymbol{0}$) and torque-free ($\boldsymbol{T =\boldsymbol{0}$) conditions.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Correspondingly, $\boldsymbol{U}$ and $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ as functions of angular coordinates, slip length, and modes of active slip velocity, can be represented mathematically as $\boldsymbol{U =\boldsymbol{U (\theta_1,\theta_2,\phi_1,\phi_2,k,\beta^A_{1m ,\beta^B_{1m ,\gamma^C_{1m ,\gamma^D_{1m )$ and $\boldsymbol{\Omega =\boldsymbol{\Omega (\theta_1,\theta_2,\phi_1,\phi_2,k,\beta^A_{1m ,\beta^B_{1m ,\gamma^C_{1m ,\gamma^D_{1m )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: In our current study, in place of their slip partitioning angle, we have four angular parameters $(\theta_1,\theta_2,\phi_1,\phi_2)$ which determine the location of the patch .

elements.sent-2411-083: As a limiting case, when $(\theta_1,\theta_2) = (0,\pi)$ and $(\phi_1,\phi_2) = (0,2\pi)$, i.e., the active slip is all over the surface of the fully covered swimmer, the corresponding velocity and rotation rate of the swimmer (with the choice $\beta^A_{11 = 0 =\beta^B_{11}$) in the laboratory frame of reference are given by \begin{eqnarray}\boldsymbol{U_s} = -\frac{2\beta^A_{10}}{3}\boldsymbol{e_3}, \label{eqn: fully_vel} \boldsymbol{\Omega_s} = -\frac{\gamma^C_{11}}{a} \boldsymbol{e_1} -\frac{\gamma^D_{11}}{a} \boldsymbol{e_2} + \frac{\gamma^C_{10}}{a} \boldsymbol{e_3}, \label{eqn: fully_rot}\end{eqnarray} which agrees with the findings by Pak et al. \cite{pak2014generalized and Burada et al. \cite{burada_pre .}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Using the velocity, pressure, and stress fields, we can estimate the viscous power dissipation of the swimmer using the relation $P = -\int_\mho \boldsymbol{n \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma \cdot \boldsymbol{u ~ dS - \int_{S-\mho \boldsymbol{n \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma \cdot \boldsymbol{u ~ dS$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-086: Here, the corresponding expressions also look cumbersome, and hence, we do not show their explicit forms.

elements.sent-2411-092: Note that this optimal patch configuration is independent of $k$ and agrees with the finding obtained by Dhar et al. \cite{dhar2019self .}
elements.sent-2411-093: We now focus on the swimmer's efficiency (refer to figure~\ref{fig: axi_vel_rot (c)).}
elements.sent-2411-094: The optimal patch configuration that moves the swimmer with maximum velocity corresponds to maximum efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-095: Figure~\ref{fig: axi_vel_rot (b) depicts the corresponding strength of the rotation rate $\Omega$ with $s_\theta$.}
elements.sent-2411-096: With increasing $s_\theta$, the active region expands, and as a result, $\Omega$ increases for any configuration of the patch.
elements.sent-2411-097: However, $\Omega$ of a swimmer with the symmetric patch never exceeds $\Omega_s$, which corresponds to the strength of the rotation rate of a fully covered swimmer.
elements.sent-2411-098: Figure~\ref{fig: axi_vel_rot (d) depicts the dissipative power $P$ with $s_\theta$.}
elements.sent-2411-099: It is evident that $P$ increases as the active region expands for any patch configuration.
elements.sent-2411-100: This is due to increase in $\Omega$ (refer to figure~\ref{fig: axi_vel_rot (b)).}

elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{Fig2.pdf} \caption{Variation of the strength of the velocity $U$ (a), the rotation rate $\Omega$ (b), the efficiency $\eta$ (c), and the dissipative power $P$ (d) of the swimmer with a symmetric patch (refer to figure~\ref{fig: schematic}(c)) as a function of $s_\theta = \theta_2 - \theta_1$ at different initial values of $\theta_1$. elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Fig3.pdf} \caption{{The flow field generated by the swimmer with a symmetric patch for the optimal patch configuration $(\theta^{o}_1,\theta^{o}_2) = (\pi/4,3\pi/4)$, in the body frame of reference. elements.sent-2411-144: After analyzing the local optimal configuration that gives maximum $\boldsymbol{U}$ for fixed $(\theta_1,\phi_1)$, {it is interesting to ask which general configuration (arbitrary active patch) leads to the maximum velocity or efficiency of the swimmer by varying $(\theta_1,\theta_2,\phi_1,\phi_2)$ for fixed $k/a$.}
elements.sent-2411-145: To answer this question, we solve an optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-146: To find the global optimal configuration, we choose the active slip parameters $(\beta^A_{11 ,\beta^B_{11 ,\beta^A_{10 )=(0,0,1)$ and $(\gamma^C_{11 ,\gamma^D_{11 ,\gamma^C_{10 )=(1,1,1)/\sqrt{3}$ as before.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-148: With this $U_{AP}$ as the objective function, we define the following optimization problem \begin{empheq [box=\fbox]{align \text{Maximize \quad U_{AP (\theta_1,\theta_2,\phi_1,\phi_2), \text{subject to, \quad 0 \leq \theta_1 \leq \theta_2 \leq \pi, \quad 0 \leq \phi_1 \leq \phi_2 \leq 2\pi.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-149: To address this, we employed numerical nonlinear global optimization tools available in Mathematica 13.2.
elements.sent-2411-150: The built-in function {NMaximize was used with prescribed constraints over the entire domain to find the global maximum.}
elements.sent-2411-151: The {NMaximize function attempts to find the global maximum of the objective function subject to the given constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Based on the nature of the objective function, {NMaximize automatically selects an in-built method such as Differential Evolution, Nelder-Mead, Random Search, or Simulated Annealing.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Since the objective function $U_{AP}$ in this study is sufficiently differentiable, the ``Differential Evolution" method can be specifically applied to find the optimal solution $(\theta_1^o, \theta_2^o, \phi_1^o, \phi_2^o)$ and the corresponding optimal value of $U_{AP}$.

elements.sent-2411-177: This work focuses on the mathematical modeling of the locomotion of self-propelled particles to explore their controllability and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-178: Now, we wish to refer to a few experimental works on swimming microorganisms and bring insights into the context of the present study.
elements.sent-2411-179: Our theoretical model also involves each parameter and hence may serve as an alternative tool to generate different physical conditions.
elements.sent-2411-180: In this context, we refer to the work by McConnell et al. \cite{mcconnell2010patchy and Xu et al. \cite{xu2011increasing , where they developed a novel method for fabricating patchy, gold-on-silica Janus particles and reported advanced synthetic techniques for preparing silica nanoparticles with rough surfaces in a reverse micro-emulsion followed by drying treatment, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-181: We try to mimic such models to establish that the results developed in this current work serve as tools to understand a larger class of physical models for which experimental results are unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-182: We consider rough-surfaced silica Janus particles with a partial gold patch coating.
elements.sent-2411-183: The typical radius of such a particle is $a \sim 300$ nm \cite{lu2003asymmetric .}
elements.sent-2411-184: We consider a hypothetical surface active slip velocity given by $(\beta^A_{11 ,\beta^B_{11 ,\beta^A_{10 )=(0,0,1.5)$ nm/s and $(\gamma^C_{11 ,\gamma^D_{11 ,\gamma^C_{10 )=a(1,1,1)/\sqrt{3}$ nm/s prescribed over the arbitrary gold patch configuration defined by $(\theta_1,\theta_2,\phi_1,\phi_2)=(\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 0, 3\pi/2)$ and rest of the surface is with slip length $k \sim 10$ nm.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Suppose that, the particle is suspended in 30 wt\% $\text{H _2\text{O _2$ solution of viscosity $\eta \sim 1.08$ mPa$\cdot$s at temperature $\text{T \sim 293$K.}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: We realize for these prescribed surface properties, the strength of the velocity of the particle with the prescribed gold patch is $\sim 84$ nm/s.
elements.sent-2411-187: Predictions based on these realistic values are likely to aid experimentalists in developing effective spherical active swimmers with an arbitrary active patch for the targeted and controlled release of drugs.
elements.sent-2411-188: In this work, we have considered a spherical swimmer of radius $a$ with an arbitrary active patch with the surface slip velocity $\boldsymbol{v_s}$ suspended in a quiescent ambient flow.
elements.sent-2411-189: The rest of the surface of the swimmer is inactive and is hydrophobic with a {Navier slip of length $k \ll a$.}
elements.sent-2411-190: The velocity $\boldsymbol{U}$, rotation rate $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$, and the flow field $\boldsymbol{u}$ (in the body frame of reference) are calculated analytically for the swimmer with a symmetric and arbitrary patch.
elements.sent-2411-191: The main objective of this work is to find an efficient optimal configuration of the patch for which the strength of the velocity is maximum.
elements.sent-2411-192: This study also illustrates how $k$, $\boldsymbol{v_s}$, and angular widths play a key role in controlling the locomotion of the swimmer.
elements.sent-2411-193: In the symmetric patch model, we find the optimal symmetric patch configuration $(\theta^{o _1,\theta^{o _2)=(\pi/4,3\pi/4)$, which is independent of $k$, gives maximum strength of the velocity.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: It is observed that for $k = 0.01$, we have $U^{o _{SP =1.409~U_s$, where $U_s$ denotes the strength of the velocity of a fully covered swimmer (activity all over the surface).}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Moreover, the swimmer is more efficient for this optimal patch configuration than any other symmetric patch configuration.
elements.sent-2411-196: In particular, $\eta^{o _{SP \approx \sqrt{2 ~\eta_s$, where $\eta_s$ denotes the efficiency of the fully covered swimmer.}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: Unlike the symmetric patch model, for the arbitrary patch model, the optimal patch configuration to achieve optimal strength of the velocity $U^{o _{AP}$ shows a dependency on $k$.}
elements.sent-2411-198: We declare that this optimal arbitrary patch configuration is globally optimal compared to symmetric and asymmetric patch models.
elements.sent-2411-200: In both patch configurations, the dissipated power of the swimmer is bounded above by the dissipated power of the fully covered swimmer.

elements.section-2411-008: Presence of hydrodynamic singularities in the vicinity of the swimmer
elements.sent-2411-205: Without loss of generality, we consider a swimmer with a symmetric patch, and over this, we set active slip's translational mode $\beta^A_{10 = \beta$ and assume that it has chirality with mode $\gamma^C_{10 = \gamma$ and all other modes are set to zero.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Consequently, applying force-free and torque-free conditions, the swimmer's velocity $\boldsymbol{U}$ and rotation rate $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ are given as \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{U} = \underbrace{-(a+3k) \frac{2\beta(3\Theta_1-\Theta_3)}{12(a+2k)+3k(3\Theta_1-\Theta_3)}}_{$u_3$}\boldsymbol{e_3}, \quad \boldsymbol{\Omega} = \underbrace{\bigg(\frac{a+3k}{a}\bigg) \frac{\gamma(9\Theta_1-\Theta_3)}{16a+3k(9\Theta_1-\Theta_3)}}_$\Omega_3$\boldsymbol{e_3}, \label{eqn: u3_o3}\end{equation} where $\Theta_i = \cos i\theta_2-\cos i\theta_1$ for $i=1,2,3$.
elements.sent-2411-207: As a result, expressions for $\boldsymbol{u _\mho$ simplifies to \begin{eqnarray}\boldsymbol{u}_{\mho} = -\boldsymbol{U}-\boldsymbol{\Omega}\times \boldsymbol{r} +\frac{a}{4r}(3u_3+2\beta)(2\cos\theta \boldsymbol{e_r}-\sin\theta\boldsymbol{e_\theta}) + \left(\frac{a^2}{r^2}\right) (a\Omega_3-\gamma) \sin\theta\boldsymbol{e_\phi}\nonumber \:- \frac{a^3}{4r^3}(u_3+2\beta)(2\cos \theta \boldsymbol{e_r}+\sin\theta\boldsymbol{e_\theta}) . \label{eqn: simp_body_flow_field_active}\end{eqnarray} It is obvious that the Stokeslet term $(\sim r^{-1 )$ and Rotlet term $(\sim r^{-2 )$ will vanish only if $u_3 = -2\beta/3$ and $\Omega_3=-\gamma/a$ respectively, which corresponds to the velocity and rotation rate components of a fully covered swimmer, as can be obtained by taking limit $(\theta_1,\theta_2) \to (0,\pi)$ on $\boldsymbol{U}$ and $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ respectively in Eqn.~\ref{eqn: u3_o3 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-208: However, in the present study, the active slip is concentrated over a specific patch region.
elements.sent-2411-209: Consequently, the swimmer’s velocity and rotation rate components are given as Eqn.~\ref{eqn: u3_o3 , and in general, $u_3 \neq -2\beta/3$ and $\Omega_3 \neq -\gamma/a$.}
